questions.prompt: Select all that are true about what the model receives after tokenization.
questions.explanation: The tokenizer converts text into stable integer IDs before the model sees it, so the model receives token IDs rather than raw words or a probability distribution.

questions.prompt: Select all objects that belong to the tokenizer-model handoff.
questions.explanation: The vocabulary mapping creates IDs, and those IDs address embedding rows. Raw browser pixels are not part of the handoff.

questions.prompt: Select all statements that are true about the tokenizer itself creating hidden-state meaning.
questions.explanation: None are true. The tokenizer makes IDs. The model layers create and edit hidden states.

questions.prompt: Order the first handoff from text into model input.
questions.explanation: Text is split into tokens, tokens map to IDs, then IDs select embedding rows.

questions.prompt: Explain why token ID 44 can appear twice but still become two different position-aware rows later.
questions.actual_answer: The same token ID selects the same learned embedding row each time, but each occurrence sits at a different sequence position. Position information is added after lookup, so identical token IDs can become different position-aware hidden-state rows.
questions.rubric: A strong answer says same ID means same embedding row, different occurrence means different position, and position information is added before transformer blocks edit the rows.
questions.hint: Mention both the embedding lookup and the position slot.

questions.prompt: Select all that are true about the tokenizer in the tokenizer-model handoff.
choices: It owns the stable mapping from text pieces to token IDs
It converts raw text into integer IDs before the model runs
It hands token IDs to the model rather than raw words
questions.explanation: All three are true. The tokenizer owns the stable text-to-ID mapping, converts text to integer IDs before the model runs, and hands those IDs to the model instead of raw words.
